This node excludes ancient sponge-iron working and treats 20th-century tungsten filaments, cemented carbides, filters, and powder-bed additive manufacturing as later applications or branches.

dependencyEdges.note: Powder metallurgy builds on prior metallurgical knowledge of metal ores, powders, heating, and working, but the scoped 1801 platinum-powder consolidation is a distinct later industrial process.

sources.title: Pure Intelligence: The Life of William Hyde Wollaston

dependencyEdges.note: Precision dies, presses, and tooling matter for reproducible industrial powder-metallurgy parts, but they are not required for Wollaston's first platinum-powder consolidation.

name: Patent Specification Drawings
description: Drawings, drafts, and model references filed with patent specifications so inventions could be distinguished, reproduced, examined, and later inspected from the public record.
scopeNote: Covers formal specification drawings, drafts, and model references filed under patent law; excludes earlier informal invention privileges without a required written disclosure package, standalone patent models, map engraving, and later mass-published patent drawing series.

sources.title: Patent Act of 1790: An Act to promote the progress of useful arts
sources.publisher: U.S. Statutes at Large reproduction via FRASER
sources.locator: Section 2 requires a written specification with a description accompanied by drafts or models, exact enough for a skilled workman to make, construct, or use the invention.

sources.title: Patent Factsheet: Drawings
sources.locator: The factsheet explains that a patent specification includes drawings referred to by the description, and that patent drawings use figures, defined lines, and reference numbers or letters.

sources.title: Patent Republic
sources.publisher: Harvard Collection of Historical Scientific Instruments
sources.locator: The exhibit notes that from the 1790 foundation of the U.S. Patent Office into the 1870s, models accompanied drawings and textual descriptions to support understanding and replicability.

sources.title: Milestones in U.S. patenting
sources.publisher: United States Patent and Trademark Office
sources.locator: USPTO dates the first U.S. patent statute to April 10, 1790, and says the first Patent Board began examining patents after passage of the act.

dependencyEdges.note: The scoped practice is a formal patent filing artifact created by statute: specifications with drafts or models were delivered to the Secretary of State as part of each patent grant.

sources.title: Patent Act of 1790: An Act to promote the progress of useful arts
sources.publisher: U.S. Statutes at Large reproduction via FRASER
sources.locator: Section 2 makes the specification, description, drafts or models, and filing requirement part of the patent-grant process.

dependencyEdges.note: Patent drawings translate an invention into referenced figures inside the specification, so technical drawing conventions enable the disclosure without making map engraving a prerequisite.

sources.title: Patent Factsheet: Drawings
sources.locator: The factsheet describes patent drawings as figures referred to by the specification and keyed to description reference numbers or letters.

name: Professional Patent Examination
description: Patent-office review of applications before grant by professional examiners, evaluating novelty, usefulness, specifications, drawings, models, prior publications, and interference claims.
scopeNote: Covers the professional U.S. Patent Office examination system created by the Patent Act of 1836; excludes the brief 1790 Patent Board examination regime, clerical registration, and generic invention privileges.

sources.title: Milestones in U.S. patenting
sources.publisher: United States Patent and Trademark Office
sources.locator: USPTO says the Patent Act of 1836 required examination of patents before granting them and established a corps of professional patent examiners.

sources.title: Inventing in Congress: Patent Law since 1790
sources.locator: National Archives describes the 1790 examination system, its 1793 replacement by clerical registration, and the 1836 creation of the U.S. Patent Office with expert evaluation of applications.

sources.title: Patent Act of 1836, Ch. 357, 5 Stat. 117
sources.locator: Sections 5-7 require recording descriptions, specifications, and drawings; filing application descriptions and drawings; models where applicable; and examination by the Commissioner before issuance.

dependencyEdges.note: Professional patent examination is an administrative layer inside a statutory patent-granting system; the 1836 Act created the Patent Office and required examination before grant.

sources.title: Milestones in U.S. patenting
sources.publisher: United States Patent and Trademark Office
sources.locator: USPTO says the 1836 act required examination before granting patents and established professional patent examiners.

dependencyEdges.note: The 1836 system depended on Patent Office custody of recorded descriptions, specifications, drawings, papers, models, and application files used in examination.

sources.title: Patent Act of 1836, Ch. 357, 5 Stat. 117
sources.locator: Sections 5-7 refer to records, papers, descriptions, specifications, drawings, models, and application files deposited and examined in the Patent Office.

dependencyEdges.note: Applications had to include drawings and written references where the invention admitted drawings, so technical drawing conventions enabled examiners to inspect and compare inventions.

sources.title: Patent Act of 1836, Ch. 357, 5 Stat. 117

name: Patent Model Collections
description: Patent Office collections of submitted invention models, drawings, and records retained for examination, restoration, public display, and later museum curation.
region: Washington, D.C. / United States Patent Office
scopeNote: Covers the U.S. Patent Office model collection and public galleries, especially the 1840 purpose-built Patent Office; excludes printed cabinet catalogs and generic invention descriptions without retained physical models.

sources.title: Milestones in U.S. patenting
sources.publisher: United States Patent and Trademark Office
sources.locator: USPTO says the first purpose-built Patent Office opened in 1840 and was the first federally funded museum collection in U.S. history, where visitors saw rows of patent models.

sources.title: Patent Models: Graphic Arts
sources.locator: Smithsonian says the U.S. Patent Office required most inventors to submit models until 1880, kept a huge collection, lost models in the 1836 fire, restored models and specifications in 1840, and later transferred models to the Smithsonian.

sources.title: American Patent Models
sources.locator: Hagley says the U.S. Patent Office required scale models with written descriptions and drawings for examination, retained submitted models, and exhibited them to large public audiences in Patent Office galleries.

dependencyEdges.note: Patent models were submitted with descriptions and drawings for Patent Office examination, giving the retained model collection its administrative purpose before public display.

sources.title: American Patent Models
sources.locator: Hagley says patent applicants submitted scale models, written descriptions, and drawings for Patent Office examination.

dependencyEdges.note: The Patent Office retained submitted models, specifications, drawings, and records, turning application artifacts into a public institutional collection.

sources.title: Patent Models: Graphic Arts
sources.locator: Smithsonian says the Patent Office became keeper of a huge collection of models and that 1840 restoration recovered models and specifications lost in the 1836 fire.

dependencyEdges.note: Public museum exhibition practice supplied the display context for Patent Office model galleries, but the patent model collection remained a specialized invention archive rather than an art museum.

sources.title: Milestones in U.S. patenting
sources.publisher: United States Patent and Trademark Office
sources.locator: USPTO says the 1840 purpose-built Patent Office was the first federally funded museum collection in U.S. history and a tourist attraction displaying rows of patent models.

name: Piezoelectric Effect
description: Conversion between mechanical stress and electric charge in certain crystals, discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie and later used in sensors, resonators, and ultrasonic transducers.

dependencyEdges.note: The piezoelectric effect is defined through electric charge produced by mechanical stress in crystals.
Chemical and materials knowledge helped identify and handle crystals that exhibit piezoelectric behavior.
Controlled measurement was needed to identify and quantify the crystal charge response.
